title: Facial pressure ENT review
source_review_basis: full authoritative documents inspected and archived; item-level retain/rewrite/remove/add comparisons recorded

assessment.text: Supports documenting symptom duration and pattern, purulent drainage, nasal obstruction, facial pain or pressure, worsening pattern, associated symptoms, and relevant negatives without asserting viral or bacterial diagnosis.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Adult Sinusitis Update
source.jurisdiction: United States specialty guideline; international evidence requiring UAE adult ENT, imaging, prescribing, and referral adaptation
source.population: Adults with acute or chronic rhinosinusitis; the official web-page target-age wording is internally inconsistent and is retained as an applicability limitation.
source.setting: Primary, urgent, outpatient, and ENT assessment and review of adult rhinosinusitis.
source.exclusions: Exact for adult acute-sinusitis symptom pattern, reassessment, chronic-rhinosinusitis objective confirmation, modifying factors, polyps, and clinician-reviewed allergy or immune testing. It does not diagnose bacterial infection, recommend antibiotics, or order imaging.

exact_location.heading: Key action statements 1A–1B — acute rhinosinusitis distinction and clinical diagnosis context

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports documenting symptom duration and pattern, purulent drainage, nasal obstruction, facial pain or pressure, worsening pattern, associated symptoms, and relevant negatives without asserting viral or bacterial diagnosis.

assessment.text: Supports documenting chronic or recurrent pattern, clinician-performed nasal examination, already available endoscopy or CT, asthma, cystic fibrosis, immunocompromise, ciliary dyskinesia, allergy or immune-testing context, and presence or absence of polyps without generating testing or treatment.
assessment.rationale: The exact chronic section supports clinician-performed ENT assessment and review of already available imaging or specialist information without interpreting CT or attributing pressure to sinus disease.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Adult Sinusitis Update
source.jurisdiction: United States specialty guideline; international evidence requiring UAE adult ENT, imaging, prescribing, and referral adaptation
source.population: Adults with acute or chronic rhinosinusitis; the official web-page target-age wording is internally inconsistent and is retained as an applicability limitation.

source.exclusions: Exact for adult acute-sinusitis symptom pattern, reassessment, chronic-rhinosinusitis objective confirmation, modifying factors, polyps, and clinician-reviewed allergy or immune testing. It does not diagnose bacterial infection, recommend antibiotics, or order imaging.

exact_location.heading: Key action statements 7A–10 — chronic distinction, objective confirmation, modifiers, testing, and polyps

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports documenting chronic or recurrent pattern, clinician-performed nasal examination, already available endoscopy or CT, asthma, cystic fibrosis, immunocompromise, ciliary dyskinesia, allergy or immune-testing context, and presence or absence of polyps without generating testing or treatment.

assessment.text: Lists high fever, frontal or periorbital swelling, severe headache, meningeal or mental-status change, visual change, and persistent unilateral or bloody symptoms.
assessment.rationale: The exact DHA red-flag section supports clinician concern, safety-net documentation, and an entered plan only when assessed; it does not infer orbital, neurological, or systemic complication.

source.title: DHA Telehealth Clinical Guidelines for Virtual Management of Acute Rhinosinusitis
source.population: People with acute rhinosinusitis symptoms; chronic, invasive fungal, and complicated disease require different pathways.
source.setting: Telehealth services in DHA-licensed health facilities.
source.exclusions: Direct for Dubai telehealth acute-rhinosinusitis documentation; partial for all chronic sinus and facial-pain causes.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Lists high fever, frontal or periorbital swelling, severe headache, meningeal or mental-status change, visual change, and persistent unilateral or bloody symptoms.
